draft.introduction: Ennead 4.3, “Problems Concerning the Soul,” examines how the soul descends into the body and whether this descent is a fall or a purposeful mission. Plotinus holds both views in tension, and this ambiguity shaped centuries of debate about embodiment.

translation.text: The soul’s descent may be compared to the way light is dispersed: the source remains undiminished while the illumination extends outward. So the soul gives life to what is below without itself departing from its higher station. It governs the body by a kind of presence, not by stooping down to the body’s level.

translator_notes.note: Plotinus’s “undescended soul” doctrine—that the highest part of the soul never fully enters the body—was controversial even among his own students. Iamblichus rejected it, insisting the soul descends entirely. The debate prefigures the Christian question of whether the image of God in humans is damaged or merely obscured by sin.
